label: Uncomplicated head lice without secondary infection
inclusion_criteria: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated scalp head lice.
No secondary skin infection, eyelash/eyebrow involvement, body lice, pubic lice/STI pathway, systemic allergic reaction, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion_criteria: Cellulitis, impetigo, abscess, pus, fever, or systemic illness.
Eyelash/eyebrow lice, body lice, pubic lice/STI pathway, treatment toxicity, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
default_follow_up: Follow up with primary care, dermatology, or school/occupational health if lice persist, contacts keep getting reinfested, or treatment is not working.

source_audit.notes: CDC supports head-lice framing, scalp predominance, medication-based treatment, and practical care measures. Autonomous Phase 1751-1800 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated scalp head lice without secondary infection, eyelash/eyebrow involvement, body or pubic lice/STI pathway, toxic treatment exposure, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, immunocompromise, or specialist-directed plan.

review.notes: Reviewed as a narrow production ontology phenotype. CDC supports head-lice framing, scalp predominance, medication-based treatment, and practical care measures. Autonomous Phase 1751-1800 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated scalp head lice without secondary infection, eyelash/eyebrow involvement, body or pubic lice/STI pathway, toxic treatment exposure, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, immunocompromise, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated scalp head lice
no secondary infection, cellulitis, eyelash lice, pubic lice, or body lice documented
no toxic treatment exposure, pregnancy, pediatric, immunocompromised, or specialist-directed pathway
